quiz.title: Unit 10 Quiz 7, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Suspensions, Colloids, Electrolytes, and Solution Preparation
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Suspensions vs Colloids, Particle Size, Electrolytes vs Nonelectrolytes, Factors Affecting Dissolution, Solution Concentration Calculations

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of Suspension?
questions.explanation: Milk of magnesia (Mg(OH)₂) is a suspension because the particles settle on standing. Albumin, soap solutions, and starch form colloidal dispersions.

questions.question: The diameter of particles in a suspension is larger than which of the following?
questions.explanation: Suspension particles are larger than 10⁻⁵ cm (100 nm). Colloidal particles range from 10⁻⁷ to 10⁻⁵ cm, and true solution particles are smaller than 10⁻⁷ cm.

questions.question: When a drop of ink from a pen is added to water, this is an example of ________
questions.explanation: Ink forms a colloidal dispersion in water. The particles are intermediate in size between true solutions and suspensions.

questions.question: In one of these compounds, the degree of temperature has little impact on solubility.
questions.explanation: NaCl solubility changes very little with temperature compared to other salts like NaNO₃ and KCl which show significant temperature dependence.

questions.question: A substance whose water solution is a poor conductor of electricity is a/an _________
questions.explanation: Nonelectrolytes do not produce ions when dissolved in water, so their solutions do not conduct electricity. Electrolytes and ionic substances conduct electricity.

questions.question: Which of the following is a nonelectrolyte?
questions.explanation: CCl₄ (carbon tetrachloride) is a molecular compound that does not ionize in water, making it a nonelectrolyte. NaCl, HCl, and KCl are electrolytes.

questions.question: When the temperature of the solvent is raised, this causes the collisions of the solvent and solute to be ___________
questions.explanation: Increasing temperature increases the kinetic energy of molecules, making collisions both more frequent and more energetic, which speeds up dissolution.

questions.question: When the surface area of the solute is increased, this would ________
options: Have no impact on dissolution rate
Make the solute become a precipitate
questions.explanation: Increasing surface area (by crushing or grinding) exposes more solute particles to the solvent, increasing the rate of dissolution.

questions.question: Substances whose water solutions are good conductors of electricity ___________
options: Require carbon for decomposition in water
Contain solute molecules that are neutral
questions.explanation: Electrolytes conduct electricity because they ionize in water, producing mobile ions that carry charge.

questions.question: The molecules of the solvent would be less mobile if _________
questions.explanation: Lowering temperature reduces the kinetic energy of molecules, making them less mobile. Stirring, pressure, and contact area affect mixing but not molecular mobility directly.

questions.question: Which of the following is a polar solvent?
questions.explanation: Ethanol is a polar solvent due to its O-H bond. Benzene, chloroform, and hexane are nonpolar or have very low polarity.

questions.question: A 0.5 M solution of NaOH is prepared by dissolving 20 g of NaOH in sufficient water to make the total volume ________.
questions.explanation:
Molar mass NaOH = 40 g/mol
Moles NaOH = 20 g / 40 g/mol = 0.5 mol
Volume = moles / M = 0.5 mol / 0.5 M = 1.0 L

questions.question: A 2 M solution of NaCl (molar mass ≈ 58.5 g/mol) is prepared by dissolving 117 g of NaCl in sufficient water to make the total volume ________.
questions.explanation:
Moles NaCl = 117 g / 58.5 g/mol = 2.0 mol
Volume = 2.0 mol / 2 M = 1.0 L

questions.question: To prepare 500 mL of a 1 M NaOH solution, a technician must dissolve ________ grams of NaOH in sufficient water.
questions.explanation:
Volume = 500 mL = 0.5 L
Moles needed = 1 M × 0.5 L = 0.5 mol
Molar mass NaOH = 40 g/mol
Mass = 0.5 mol × 40 g/mol = 20 g

questions.question: A 1 M solution of CaCl₂ (molar mass ≈ 111 g/mol) is prepared by dissolving 111 g of CaCl₂ in sufficient water to make the total volume ________.
questions.explanation:
Moles CaCl₂ = 111 g / 111 g/mol = 1.0 mol
For a 1 M solution, volume = 1.0 L

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered suspensions, colloids, electrolytes, and solution preparation! 🧪📊
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the differences between suspensions and colloids and electrolyte concepts.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on factors affecting dissolution and solution concentration calculations.
scoreMessages.0: Please review suspension vs colloid, electrolyte vs nonelectrolyte, and molarity calculations.
